summary: Jeff Atwood explains the origin of his online handle 'Wumpus,' tracing it back to the classic text-based game 'Hunt the Wumpus.' He describes how playing the TI 99/4a cartridge version around 1985 was one of his formative computing experiences. The post provides historical context about the game's origins, noting Gregory Yob created the original BASIC version in 1975. Atwood also shares that a colleague ported the classic game to C#/.NET, preserving the original text-based UI while modernizing some technical aspects like using XML for cave maps.
key_points: The 'Wumpus' handle is part of Atwood's deliberate personal branding strategy, connected to a meaningful computing memory
Hunt the Wumpus on the TI 99/4a was a formative experience on his first 'real' computer
The game has deep roots in computing history, with the original BASIC version dating to 1975
A .NET port preserves the classic gameplay while updating technical implementation details
money_quotes: It was one of my formative computing experiences on the first 'real' computer I owned.
If you use your wits and your arrows judiciously, you might find that Wumpus one day.
key_insight: Personal branding in tech can be rooted in formative computing experiences that connect us to the history and culture of programming.
